title: Cloud-Native Patterns
summary: 12-Factor App, cloud-native design principles, FinOps cost optimisation, and building cloud-agnostic services.

sections.content:
## The 12-Factor App

The 12-Factor methodology (Heroku, 2011) defines best practices for building cloud-native, scalable, maintainable SaaS applications.

| Factor | Principle |
|---|---|
| **I. Codebase** | One codebase tracked in VCS, many deploys |
| **II. Dependencies** | Explicitly declare and isolate dependencies |
| **III. Config** | Store config in environment (not code) |
| **IV. Backing services** | Treat backing services (DB, cache) as attached resources |
| **V. Build/Release/Run** | Strictly separate build, release, and run stages |
| **VI. Processes** | Execute app as stateless processes |
| **VII. Port binding** | Export services via port binding |
| **VIII. Concurrency** | Scale out via process model |
| **IX. Disposability** | Fast startup, graceful shutdown |
| **X. Dev/prod parity** | Keep dev, staging, prod as similar as possible |
| **XI. Logs** | Treat logs as event streams |
| **XII. Admin processes** | Run admin tasks as one-off processes |

sections.title: 12-Factor Config & Backing Services
sections.content:
graph LR
    subgraph Bad - Config in Code
        App1[App] -->|hardcoded| DB1["jdbc:postgresql://prod-db:5432"]
    end
    subgraph Good - Config from Environment
        App2[App] -->|ENV: DATABASE_URL| DB2[Any DB]
        App2 -->|ENV: REDIS_URL| Cache[Any Cache]
        App2 -->|ENV: KAFKA_BROKERS| Queue[Any Queue]
    end
    style App2 fill:#059669,color:#fff
    style App1 fill:#dc2626,color:#fff

## Graceful Shutdown

Cloud-native apps must handle `SIGTERM` gracefully — finishing in-flight requests, closing DB connections, deregistering from service discovery:

```java
// Spring Boot handles this automatically with:
# application.properties
server.shutdown=graceful
spring.lifecycle.timeout-per-shutdown-phase=30s

# Kubernetes must send SIGTERM before SIGKILL
# spec.terminationGracePeriodSeconds: 60  (default 30)
```

### Kubernetes pod termination sequence:
```
1. Pod set to Terminating
2. K8s removes from Service endpoints (no new traffic)
3. K8s sends SIGTERM to container
4. App stops accepting new requests, finishes in-flight ones
5. After terminationGracePeriodSeconds → SIGKILL (if still running)
```

**Gap problem**: steps 2 and 3 happen concurrently — new requests might still arrive after SIGTERM. Solution: add a pre-stop sleep hook:

```yaml
lifecycle:
  preStop:
    exec:
      command: ["sleep", "5"]  # wait for LB to drain
```

## FinOps — Cloud Cost Optimisation

FinOps is the practice of bringing financial accountability to cloud spending. Key practices:

### Right-sizing
```bash
# Kubernetes: check actual usage vs requested
kubectl top pods -n production
# If actual: cpu=50m, memory=128Mi
# But request: cpu=500m, memory=512Mi
# → You're over-provisioning and over-paying by 10x
```

### Spot/Preemptible Instances
- AWS Spot, GCP Preemptible: 60-80% cheaper than on-demand
- Can be terminated at any time → only for stateless workloads
- Use node affinity to schedule batch/non-critical workloads on spot

### Cluster Autoscaler
```yaml
# Scale down unused nodes automatically
cluster-autoscaler:
  scale-down-utilization-threshold: 0.5  # node <50% utilized → scale down
  scale-down-delay-after-add: 10m
  scale-down-unneeded-time: 10m
```

### KEDA — Event-driven Autoscaling
Scale pods to **zero** when idle, scale up from Kafka lag, queue depth, etc.:

```yaml
apiVersion: keda.sh/v1alpha1
kind: ScaledObject
spec:
  scaleTargetRef:
    name: order-processor
  minReplicaCount: 0    # scale to zero!
  maxReplicaCount: 50
  triggers:
  - type: kafka
    metadata:
      topic: orders
      lagThreshold: "100"  # 1 pod per 100 messages lag
```

sections.title: Cloud Cost Optimisation Layers
sections.content:
graph TD
    Savings[Cost Savings Opportunities]
    Savings --> RightSize[Right-sizing\nMatch requests to actual usage]
    Savings --> Spot[Spot Instances\n60-80% cheaper for stateless]
    Savings --> ScaleZero[Scale-to-Zero\nKEDA for batch workloads]
    Savings --> Reserved[Reserved Instances\n30-60% discount for committed use]
    Savings --> Storage[Storage Tiering\nMove old data to cheaper tiers]
    RightSize --> VPA[Vertical Pod Autoscaler\nAuto-adjust resource requests]
    style Savings fill:#4f46e5,color:#fff
    style ScaleZero fill:#059669,color:#fff

sections.question: Why should a pre-stop sleep hook be added to Kubernetes pods?
options: To give the application time to save its state before shutdown
To compensate for the race condition where load balancer endpoint removal and SIGTERM happen concurrently, preventing traffic to a shutting-down pod
To allow Kubernetes to complete health checks before termination
To ensure database connections are properly closed
sections.explanation: Kubernetes removes the pod from Service endpoints AND sends SIGTERM at the same time. The load balancer may take a few seconds to propagate the endpoint removal. Without a pre-stop sleep, the pod can receive new requests after it starts shutting down. A 5-second sleep gives the LB time to drain before the app starts rejecting connections.
